So it's looking at what's going on with your client, and then thinking instead of what needs to be removed, what needs to be stopped, what could be added in to strengthen, to make the person happier, to make their relationships better, just by adding something. And sometimes what you're adding has nothing to do with the presenting problem. It comes from...you know, it's actually an Ericksonian principle, which is it's much easier to add something to a behavior than to take it away. That's the easiest way to change behavior. And also as a coach, you'll often be talking to someone and they'll tell you about their challenges and their ambitions, and then you'll get the feeling that something is really missing, that maybe sometimes people get really obsessed with a certain kind of problem so they can completely block out another whole area of life. So they'll have some people who will work on...you know, they'll be so focused on work that they've just forgotten that even they should ever have a relationship. So one SI strategy would be pretend strategy, meaning making the deliberate a behavior which is otherwise looked at as unconscious or uncontrollable. So if a husband and a wife and the husband has an anxiety attack, then he can pretend to have the anxiety attack and the wife can be with him and they can have all the benefits of having an anxiety attack without actually having one. They're testing to see what most affects people and their decisions to be altruistic to a homeless person or whether to take care of them or whether to ignore them. It was somebody just lying there looking very sick. And the ministers were late for where they had to go. So they watched to see what are the conditions that really changed the person's behavior. And then the other thing would be that they were just told a story about someone who helped someone. And they're seeing what will make this person be... And the third thing is that they're just late. And it turns out that being late for their talk was the most important factor for whether they just step over the person who's lying there sick or whether they stop and help them. So one is the archetype strategy where we help the client to find the parts of themselves that are super wise or very strong like the warrior or magical or very soft and nurturing like the lover. Or we help them get grounded. What we're doing is we're taking these parts that are inside of all of us and we're having a conversation with them and asking for their opinions and then helping the different parts of a person have a conversation and get together, get integrated and focused on how to overcome whatever the client's question is. So before doing that strategy, and this is the one minute version of that strategy, we like to always ask, I like to always have my clients ask a question that they will ask all those different parts of themselves that same question. So Mark reminded me of a session we had during one of our teachings where a woman presented, Mark, I can't remember what the problem was. And as soon as she did that she was then able to talk about that behavioral problem or fear that she had in a different way because she had just, in her own real experience, transformed it through this way of talking about it as a metaphor. Yeah, yeah. Because it was a fear, it was a way she felt about it and when you change it through a metaphor you're changing the feeling. So you're actually changing the thing. Another example is we talked to a student, I think it was a week ago, maybe two weeks ago, a mother of a four-year-old who was overwhelmed by the little girl's behavior where the mother was doing something and the child would interrupt her and if the mother would not be interrupted the child would scream and cry and then the mother had to console her and so on. And we suggested that the mother initiate that game, that the mother say, let's play that I'm doing the laundry and you call me and I say I can't come now and then you pretend to cry and scream and then I come and I pretend to cry and scream and we're both screaming. Something like that, I don't remember the details, is changing a mother-child relationship that are stuck in an unpleasant pattern and making it a game. Right, so that's creating the greater possibilities that the stuck pattern now becomes a playful happy pattern. Right, it's now a game. What if every time you felt like fighting instead it made you realize that you felt very sexy? That would be another example. So what are the possibilities of the ways that we can work with our patterns or emotions or blocks to transform them? That's what this stage is all about. Yeah, and very often the biggest problem with someone's pattern is they feel like it's the only one. So I was like, do I don't, I eat ice cream. I was fighting this one thing rather than seeing the possibilities, all the different ways of feeling better in that moment or all the different things that are possible. So just the option of having many choices is important there. Right, and you know talking about many choices, one of my favorite strategies for dealing with food cravings is the putting it off. So instead of denying someone that desire for the treat, you put it off until the next day. So you never say, no I can't have that. And this comes from the concept of Chloe and Mark, you guys will remember the name of that kind of conscious eating or something like that. Yeah, kind of conscious eating. Yeah, where people fall into different types with their behavior problem around food. So, but what helps most of these types is just to say, tomorrow I can have that. And as soon as you've given yourself that ability to have that thing the next day, a huge energy is released of that craving or desire. So this is very different than using shame or willpower. Instead it's knowing that you can do that the next day. So that's something that you can apply to all different types of behaviors. That's great. It probably shifted to like the anticipatory part of the brain that's pleasurable. So it's, you know, you're still looking forward to it. Right. Yeah, great. Okay, so seventh stage, the final one. Yes. Would you like to go ahead, Martha? Sure. So SI stage, the seventh stage is the three A's, accept, anchor, and anticipate challenges. So again, we're talking specifically right now about changing a behavior or pattern. So when a client sees a new perspective or a self or experience, you want to accept it. You want to accept, you know, you want them to accept and love and take an experience as part of themselves and know that they're different from the behavior of the pattern that they're suffering from. Anchoring is about anchoring your new self, behavior, your relationships, your priorities, and your purpose, and anticipating challenges about who could my new, you know, my new breakthrough, who could this threaten, or when will I still need my old coping or behavior pattern, and how will I still give and love myself, even if I fall into it, or how will I cope with my life in a different way? So, and we actually had a question here. Someone wanted to know a little bit more about anchoring. Would you like to add about that in this kind of situation? Well, I think it's about anchoring when you think of an anchor going into the sand to keep a ship still in the ocean. Yes. When we're anchoring in ourselves, we're anchoring the knowledge, and this is part of acceptance, that we can be more than this. Or we are already more than this. We are already the person who no longer has that issue. Does that make sense, Mark? Absolutely. And so, you want to always, when you have a...when the person transcends their problem, or they see different creative possibilities, or when they see...or they accept their way that they are, and they don't...they're not beating themselves up anymore, you want to anchor that in, meaning that should be their new reference point in the future. And then... And this could be done with a name. So, the person has a new name. This could be done with an archetype. So, you know that that wise woman is inside of you. And you just have to sometimes invite her to be the most important one. There's so many different ways to anchor, and sometimes you see people...sometimes you see Tony using a touch on the shoulder. And that's a little bit different than what we're talking about right now. Yeah. But the basic idea of anchoring is that you're reminding yourself about something. You're basically giving yourself a little...the anchoring is like you said, it's in the sand. It's like...it's a metaphor for tapping into and reminding yourself of something being there, and giving yourself a handle for it. So, another example would be somebody who is no longer going to do a certain behavior, that every time they wash their hands, they'll be reminded that they have a new behavior. Mm-hmm. So, washing their hands becomes an anchor for their new belief in themselves. Yep. It also could be...it also could be the figure of somebody in their past. For example, a grandparent, a schoolteacher who believed in them in a very positive way. And so, the anchor could be going back to think about that person who believed in them. Absolutely. And people will use you, the coach, also as an anchor sometimes. Oh, yeah. Those things to you...I heard your voice remind me. Exactly. I heard...I remembered something you said, and sometimes that's when sessions work weeks into the future. We have seven minutes, so we'll just run quickly through this, just reading out the slides to you in case you're listening in. So, this is where we applied the seven SI stages to the second coaching path, which is helping person to achieve their outcome and their goals. So, the first stage is understanding what you need to understand. So, when you're helping somebody with a goal or an outcome, for instance, their career, their weight, their health, there anything... And it usually involves kind of the participation of the external world or the physical world somehow. Then you need to... What you want to understand is you want to help them with reverse engineering. So, in other words, one of the biggest, one of the most important metaphors and tools for helping someone with an outcome or goal is thinking from the goal backwards. Like, say that you've already achieved it, you're here. You know, if you wanted to go to Paris, okay, so we're in Paris now. How did we get to Paris? Okay, we got in a flight, right? And we got a hotel room. How did we get the flight in the hotel room? We earned the money, we scheduled it. So, how do we earn the money, we scheduled it. So, you're working backwards to all the things that would require... You would... That are requirements to get to Paris, right? So, that's one of the things that you want to use as a goal. As a... Sorry, as a tool. As a tool in your first stage when working with that. Yeah. So, when you want to understand a person... It's like you're creating a map for getting to the outcome or goal and you're doing that by working backwards from the location on the map that you get to your destination back to when you began on that map. Yeah. And you can be the client's hand and write it out for them. You can use large pieces of paper to plan out the journey. So, the other thing you want to understand are what is in the way. So, what are the obstacles? Are there behaviors, beliefs, relationships, rules, and fears that are in the way? And what are the... What's the truth in the client's self-criticism? So, how... You want to assess the severity of a problem. Sometimes someone will say, you know what? I'm getting this business started in the night. I just can't believe how I procrastinate. And for one person, it's that they haven't even done step one of starting a business. And for the other person, they've done 99% and they're beating themselves up because they didn't do their accounting right. Right? And so, you want to assess to see, well, how close is this person to the goal? And not to the goal, but how are they close or how much action are they actually taking so far? So, that would be the first stage of understanding. Great. So, second stage reframe. Coach brings a client a new focus and context for their goal and outcome. So, the purpose is to attain a goal, but in a doable phases and steps. And remember, we're talking about outcome and goal-based client coaching with strategic intervention. So, you look at the lifecycle, career cycle, life stage. You look at the relationship cycle that your client's in. So, for example, their goal might be to get married, but right now they're single. So, what comes between being single and getting married? They may want to have a great relationship with a teenage child, but now they're fighting five times a day. So, what goes between fighting constantly and having a great relationship? When you're reframing at this stage in the coaching, you're going to use the six human needs to understand, is there another way for your client to meet the need behind the present goal right now? So, if the goal is to make money, the need would be certainty. So, how can they make sure to meet their need for certainty every day, even before they make more money? Understanding the client's strengths versus the client's interests. So, sometimes people think that their strengths are their interests, and they don't really understand what their strengths are. So, you want to get detailed about what is special about that person, what would their friends say about them. This is building up a bunch of information that the client has at their disposal to remind themselves of what makes themselves strong and special. And that, having that allows them to have a reframe which makes their goal or their outcome much more doable. So, another example of this is the client says that their goal is to publish a book. And so, the reframe would be that the client needs to write at least two pages every day. So, the new goal is how is that person going to write two pages every day? It's shifted into a very doable daily goal. Great. And so, the purpose of the second stage reframe when it comes to outcome-based coaching is that people will often have a general sense of urgency or an emotional ambition or excitement. And it's very vague, and it depends on things that are out of their control. Like, I'm going to be a best-selling author. So, a lot of things have to happen in the world for you to become a best-selling author. So, as a coach, you want to bring them back to actionable steps with phases that they can meet their goal, outcome in parts every day. And the reframe is one of the ways that you want to help the person really assess what action is going to help take them there. And with action-based coaching, there's a lot more focus on results in the world. And so, you kind of want to help to rein them in and help them do stuff that's in their control. And here is the third is action, which is what SI strategies you're going to use. So, we've talked a bit about all of these in different classes, but just to run through them quickly, if you're listening and not looking, swallow the frog. That's doing something, first thing in the morning, getting the hardest thing done right away that you need to do towards your outcome. Reverse engineering, which Mark talked about using the example of Paris. Memory modification. So, overcoming any negative thoughts or memories that the client relives as an excuse to not do what they need to do. This can happen as you get into the details and the beliefs that your client can meet their goal. They will usually come up with a list for you of all the reasons they can't. The things they think about, the things they remember, their rules, their beliefs. So, you want to change that by confusing the thoughts and memories. And you can do that in a fun way. I think we've given you examples using silly voices and black and white images and moving quickly or very slowly so that you go back into those thoughts and memories and you create a shift. And they really can't go back there. You do that in a session and you have them practice that and they won't have those negative incantations or memories or beliefs anymore. Rewards. You really, really believe in rewards. So, you want to help your client have small rewards every day for the small actions they take towards their goal. And so, the other thing is again, because it's outcome-based, so it depends on the world and participation and understanding how things work. And another strategy is to have them interview other people who have attained similar goals or outcomes. It's important for your client to be getting good information to have good relationships with people who are achieving or involved with the types of achievements that they want for themselves. And you know, this is something we often think of younger people doing, but this works with anyone. To seek out, even with goals like, I want to have a happier marriage, ask your client, find people for your client to interview who are happily married. There's nothing better than hearing it from several different people what they did. Great. So, okay, next one. All right. So, four stages expanding the unit. So, again, when people are outcome-oriented, they want to achieve something, it's important that they're not alone. So, you can help them mobilize with people in their lives. So, one thing is to dedicate the goal. So, like, if you have a goal or an outcome, people will say, you know, I really want to do this. Well, who is this for? Who else will benefit from you doing this? Or who from your past will be proud of you for doing this? And how will it help other people? So, you want to expand this. It's just not about you, because one thing about when we make something just about us, it becomes, you know, prone to secondary gain. It becomes just about us and our brain and meeting our own needs. When other people are involved, we tend to get more organized and more responsible, and we feel more accountable. The other thing is to help the person to get a mentor or a growth community that's specialized in the type of achievement that they want. They want to become a business person. They want to become a healer. They want to become an herbalist. They want to become a medical provider. I'm getting this fucking right here in terms of my examples. I have a funny one for this. I don't know. I'm on the health kick here, but Dr. Ladd is the advisor. He's a famous Ayurvedic doctor. That if you want to be skinny, you should hang out with skinny people. So, I always thought that was so funny when I read that in one of his books. But it's an easy thing to remember that you want to be around the people who have that thing that you're going towards. So, if you want to be a runner, go join a group of runners and hear their stories. You want to be an artist. Find a community of artists. So, the other parts of expanding the unit is that you can help the person get an accountability partner. If this could be you or it could be someone else who they can report to, you want to, again, you want to help them mobilize with other people. And you also want to understand, in terms of expanding the unit, you want to understand the key players in that person's life. And you want to understand the type of roadblocks that might be in their way. Some people have, they come from cultural backgrounds that are going to create drag on their making progress in a certain direction. They might have relationships where people don't support or don't agree with them in certain areas. They might have family or personal fears of success or cultural stereotypes that stand in the way. So, very often, for instance, you have the problem of the young executive and their father is a blue collar guy who thinks that people in suits are evil. Or you have the professional woman whose mother had a very kind of sad life where her possibilities were really restricted. And so, how do you do with the guilt or the attachment to the person from the previous generation and continue to make progress and reconcile that with the life they had to live? And you would do this actually by also by calling in some of those people into a coaching conversation. Yes. And helping elevate the relationship so that in the example of the parents, they could actually tell their adult child how proud of them they are. Yeah, and give their support. Yeah, because often these aren't real fears. Yes. They get up fears that a person uses to keep themselves from gaining too much success because they're just afraid to be too different from where they came from or who their parents were. Yep. Or they want to feel that sense of compassion and I don't know if compassion is the right word, but they want to be more like their family. So, they don't do the thing that they also want, which is very different from their family. They're afraid they won't belong anymore to that code. Right. Yeah. So, the next stage, adding. So, if an action makes a person feel guilty, it isn't an adding activity. We already talked about that quite a bit. We think of adding as down to earth simple actions which give strength, happiness, balance, and inspiration. So, making new friends, learning a new language, incorporating relaxation techniques or meditation, entertaining activities into a day or a week. So, these can be very simple suggestions you make that point out to your client how important it is for them to have greater happiness or greater peace of mind. And you're anchoring at the same time as you're adding. You're letting the client know how important it is to include more into their life. And again, as a coach, you don't want to be shy about adding. You're helping them with their outcome and then you think, you know what, I think this is really going to also support your outcome. Getting a little bit of exercise every day, that kind of thing. And it's a great way for a coach to be able to, you know, you're going to spot things that are missing. And by, instead of switching it and replacing what they want, you can add a little bit of that to what they're already saying that they want. And again, the example of exercise might be hard for some people. So, instead it might be just changing your environment. So, I think it would really help you to get to your goal if several times a day you changed your environment instead of working in one room. That might be taking a walk. That might be going to a gym. That might be going to a cafe. That might be calling up a friend. But you need to change your environment. That would be a way of adding. Giving many choices so you're also brainstorming, but you're really increasing the possibilities, which is the next stage. That was a strategy. It's funny. There was a high university administrator who was really getting sick of his work, all of his administrative work. So, often when people are in a very stressful environment, whatever it is, taking those breaks, I know of one woman that would go into the bathroom for long periods of time to get a break from her toddlers. You want to help them to do some, you can do some exercises. People are getting afraid. So, with this, with an outcome-based coaching, the tendency is for the person to either over-focus on their outcome so much that they starve every part of their life, or they get perfectionistic and they get detail-oriented and they spend all their time doing some very basic things, or they get really tired really quickly. And basically, it's helping the person keep perspective, helping them stay sharp, and do the proper amount of work that's strategically intelligent to move forward. So, the three A's in this are really about helping the person's focus. So, what is great and more important than this goal or outcome?
